passages.title: Multitasking
passages.source_html:
Multitasking


Driving, eating, talking on the phone, sending emails...in today's busy world most of us find ourselves doing two or three things at once. Ours is a multitasking, multi-achieving society - or is it?


A


Scientific research has identified a bottleneck in our brains that could suggest we are fundamentally incapable of true multitasking. If experimental findings reflect real-world performance, people who think they are multitasking are probably just underperforming in all of their parallel pursuits.


B


The problem, according to René Marois, a psychologist at Vanderbilt University in Tennessee, is that there's a sticking point in the brain. In order to locate this, Marois asked volunteers to watch a screen and press a key with a specific finger when a particular image appeared. Then, in a separate activity, they learned to listen to different recordings and to respond by making a specific sound. In both tasks, typical response time was about half a second, and volunteers quickly reached their peak performance. The trouble came when Marois showed participants an image, then almost immediately played them a sound. It appeared that if the second task is introduced within the half-second or so it takes to process and react to the first, it will simply be delayed until the first one is done. Delays progressively shorten as the interval between presenting the tasks lengthens.


C


There are at least three points where we seem to get stuck, says Marois. The first is simply identifying what we're looking at. This can take a few tenths of a second, during which crucial window of concentration we are incapable of seeing and recognising a second item. This limitation is known as the attentional blink. A second limitation is our short-term visual memory. This capacity shortage is thought to explain, in part, our astonishing inability to detect even huge changes in scenes that are otherwise identical, so-called change blindness. A third limitation is that choosing a response also takes brainpower. Selecting a response to one stimulus will hold back by some tenths of a second your ability to respond to a parallel one. This is called the response selection bottleneck theory, first proposed in 1952, and elaborated on by Marois and his colleagues in 2006.


D


But David Meyer of the University of Michigan has other ideas. Known as something of an optimist by his peers, Meyer has written papers with titles like 'Virtually perfect time-sharing in dual-task performance'. His experiments have shown that with enough practice at least 200 tries-some people can execute two tasks simultaneously as competently as if they were doing them one after another. Not all people manage this harmonious time-share, however. Meyer argues that individual differences come down to variations in the character of the processor-some brains are just more 'cautious', some more 'daring'. And contrary to popular belief, there are no noticeable difference between men and women. Meyer believes that it's not a central bottleneck which causes dual-task 'interference' but rather adaptive executive control which 'schedules task processes appropriately to obey instructions about their relative priorities and serial order'.


E


Marois's volunteers have also shown a huge improvement at managing concurrent tasks through regular practice. Where he disagrees with Meyer is in what the brain is doing to achieve this. Marois speculates that practice might give us the chance to find less congested circuits to execute a task – like finding trusty back streets to avoid heavy traffic on main roads – effectively making our response to the task subconscious. But while some dual tasks benefit from practice, others simply do not, according to Pierre Jolicoeur of the University of Montreal. Dual tasks involving visual stimulus and a motor response* (which he dubs 'in the eye and out the hand') and auditory stimulus with a verbal response (in the ear and out the mouth) can, with enough training, be performed as well together as apart. He speculates that the brain connections they use may be somehow special, because we learn to speak by hearing and learn to move by looking. But pair visual input with verbal response, or sound to motor, and there's no such dramatic improvement.


F


For research purposes, these experiments have to be kept simple Real-world multitasking poses much greater challenges. Needless to say, there appear to be no researchers who believe that you can safely drive and have a phone conversation. And according to results or from a study at the University of Utah, even using a hands-free phone does not improve a driver's response time. The study confirms that what distracts a driver so badly is the very act of talking to someone who isn't present in the car and therefore is unaware of the hazards facing the driver. And the general preconception that we tend to get worse at multitasking as we age is borne out. In one study conducted by Art Kramer at the University of Illinois. for example, both young and old participants were asked to do a simulated driving task while carrying on a conversation. He found that while younger drivers tended to miss background changes, older drivers failed to notice things that were highly relevant as well, and likewise had more trouble paying attention to the more important parts of the scene.


G


However, Kramer also found that older people improved their performance with practice, and that brain scans showed that underlying that improvement was a change in the way their brains became active. While it's clear that practice can often make a difference, the basic facts remain sobering. For most of our history, says Marois, we probably never needed to do more than one thing at a time, so we haven't evolved to be able to. 'We have this impression of an almighty, complex brain,' he says, 'and yet we have very humbling and crippling limits.'


*motor response: a response that involves a muscular movement

groups.title: Questions 27-40
groups.source_html:
Questions 27 – 31


Choose the correct answer.

27

The main purpose of René Marois's experiment was to establish

正确答案： B. the stage at which people become too overloaded with input to respond efficiently.

Q27, B

定位关键信息：main purpose, René Marois's experiment


原文：B段, “The problem, according to René Marois, a psychologist at Vanderbilt University in Tennessee, is that there's a sticking point in the brain. In order to locate this, Maris asked volunteers to watch a screen and press a key with a specific finger when a particular image appeared. Then, in a separate activity, they learned to listen to different recordings and to respond by making a specific sound.”


解析：原文提到René Marois的观点，他认为我们无法一心二用的原因是大脑的局限性(sticking point)。为了找到大脑的局限性，René Marois进行了的相关实验, 因此答案为B选项。


同义替换：purpose=in order to

28

What does David Meyer say about dual-task performance?

正确答案： D. Success at dual-task activities depends on the person concerned.

Q28, D

定位关键信息：David Meyer, dual-task


原文：D段, “His experiments have shown that with enough practice at least 200 tries – some people can execute two tasks simultaneously as competently as if they were doing them one after another. Not all people manage this harmonious time-share, however. Meyer argues that individual differences come down to variations in the character of the processor – some brains are just more 'cautious', some more 'daring'.”


解析：原文提到实验表明在练习超过200次后, 有些人能够像按顺序完成两个任务一样同时处理好两个任务。David Meyer认为这种个体间的差异取决于处理任务的人的性格, 对应D选项。


同义替换：dual=two, success=competently, person=processor

29

On what point do Marois and Meyer agree?

正确答案： C. the value of repetition in improving performance in dual-task situations

Q29, C

定位关键信息：point, Marois and Meyer agree


原文：E段, “Marois's volunteers have also shown a huge improvement at managing concurrent tasks through regular practice.”


解析：原文提到参与Marois研究的志愿者在进行了练习后, 处理同时进行的任务的表现有明显提升，对应C选项。


同义替换：repetition=practice, dual tasks=concurrent tasks

30

According to Pierre Jolicoeur, the kind of dual tasks which can benefit from practice are those involving

正确答案： A. combinations which mirror the way people acquire basic skills.

Q30, A

定位关键词：Pierre Jolicoeur, benefit, practice


原文：E段, “But while some dual tasks benefit from practice, others simply do not, according to Pierre Jolicoeur of the University of Montreal. Dual tasks involving visual stimulus and a motor response (which he dubs 'in the eye and out the hand') and auditory stimulus with a verbal response (in the ear and out the mouth) can, with enough training, be performed as well together as apart.”


解析：原文提到Jolicoeur 认为练习对于同时完成有些任务有所帮助，比如涉及视觉输入与运动神经反应, 听觉刺激与言语反应相结合的任务, 对应A选项。


同义替换： combinations=visual stimulus and a motor response/auditory stimulus with a verbal response

31

In his dual-task experiments, Art Kramer observed that, compared with younger participants, older people

正确答案： B. found it harder to concentrate on key aspects of driving.

Q31, B

定位关键信息：Art Kramer, younger, older


原文：F段, “In one study conducted by Art Kramer at the University of Illinois, for example, both young and old participants were asked to do a simulated driving task while carrying on a conversation. He found that while younger drivers tended to miss background changes, older drivers failed to notice things that were highly relevant as well, and likewise had more trouble paying attention to the more important parts of the scene.”


解析：原文提到老年人在一边聊天一边开车时无法注意到和驾驶高度相关的事物, 并且更难注意到更加重要的事物, 对应B选项。


同义替换：had more trouble paying attention to=harder to concentrate on

Questions 32 – 35


Complete each sentence with the correct ending. Choose the correct ending and move it into the gap.

Options (Word Bank):
A. the delay in deciding on an appropriate reaction to a stimulus.
B. variations between people in their ability to respond to dual-task situations.
C. the period when we are processing input and therefore unable to take in further information.
D. a mechanism which places task in sequence according to their perceived importance.
E. a failure to identify differences between similar images.
Question / ParagraphABCDE
32

The phrase 'attentional blink' describes ________
✅ 正确答案：C

Q32, C

定位关键词：attentional blink


原文：C段, “The first is simply identifying what we're looking at. This can take a few tenths of a second, during which crucial window of concentration we are incapable of seeing and recognising a second item. This limitation is known as the attentional blink.”


解析：原文提到attentional blink是指当我们需要花几十分之一秒来确定所看到物体是什么, 在这个注意力的空窗期内我们无法看到和分辨另一个物体, 对应C选项。


同义替换：period=window, incapable=unable, seeing and recognising a second item=to take in further information.
33

Scientists talk about 'change blindness' to denote ________
✅ 正确答案：E


定位关键词：change blindness

 

原文：C段, “A second limitation is our short-term visual memory. This capacity shortage is thought to explain, in part, our astonishing inability to detect even huge changes in scenes that are otherwise identical, so-called change blindness. ”

 

解析：原文提到change blindness是指由于短期视觉记忆的限制, 我们无法识别相似图像之间的差异, 对应E选项

 

同义替换：failure=inability, identify=detect, differences=changes, similar=identical
34

The idea of the 'response selection bottleneck' was suggested as an explanation for ________
✅ 正确答案：A

定位关键词：response selection bottleneck


原文：C段, “Selecting a response to one stimulus will hold back by some tenths of a second your ability to respond to a parallel one. This is called the response selection bottleneck theory, …”


解析：原文提到response selection bottleneck是指选择对一个刺激的做出反应会导致对同时发生另一个刺激的反应延迟几十分之一秒, 对应A选项。


同义替换：delay=hold back, reaction=respond, one指代stimulus
35

Meyer uses the term 'adaptive executive control' to refer to ________
✅ 正确答案：D

定位关键词：adaptive executive control


原文：D段, “Meyer believes that it's not a central bottleneck which causes dual-task 'interference' but rather adaptive executive control which 'schedules task processes appropriately to obey instructions about their relative priorities and serial order'.”


解析：原文提到Meyer认为adaptive executive control会根据任务的相对优先级和顺序来合理安排任务过程, 对应D选项


同义替换：places task in sequence=schedule task processes, importance=priorities

Questions 36 – 40


Choose YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer, choose NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer, or choose NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this.

36

In the scientific community, Meyer's theories receive less support than those of Marois.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

Q36, NOT GIVEN

定位关键词：Meyer's theories, those of Marois


原文：D段, “But David Meyer of the University of Michigan has other ideas. Known as something of an optimist by his peers, Meyer has written papers with titles like 'Virtually perfect time-sharing in dual-task performance'. His experiments have shown that with enough practice at least 200 tries-some people can execute two tasks simultaneously as competently as if they were doing them one after another. Not all people manage this harmonious time-share, however. Meyer argues that individual differences come down to variations in the character of the processor-some brains are just more 'cautious', some more 'daring'. And contrary to popular belief, there are no noticeable difference between men and women. Meyer believes that it's not a central bottleneck which causes dual-task 'interference' but rather adaptive executive control which 'schedules task processes appropriately to obey instructions about their relative priorities and serial order'.”


解析：原文提到David Meyer和Rene Marois两位科学家对多任务处理的不同观点。Meyer认为通过足够练习, 人们可以同时执行两项任务。而Marois则认为大脑的瓶颈限制了我们真正同时处理多个任务的能力。原文并未提及科学界对哪种理论的支持更多, 因此答案为NOT GIVEN。

37

It is commonly thought that gender plays a significant role in determining dual-task performance.

正确答案： A. YES

Q37, YES

定位关键词：gender


原文：D段, “And contrary to popular belief, there are no noticeable difference between men and women.”


解析：原文提到实验结果与流行的观点不同, 实验表明男性和女性在同时完成两个任务时的表现没有明显差异。由此可以推断流行的观点认为男性和女性在同时完成两个任务时的表现存在明显差异, 答案为YES。


同义替换：commonly=popular, belief=is thought, gender=men and women, significant=noticeable

38

Jolicoeur's experiments on dual-task competence took place under laboratory conditions.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

Q38, NOT GIVEN

定位关键词：Jolicoeur's experiments


原文：E段, “But while some dual tasks benefit from practice, others simply do not, according to Pierre Jolicoeur of the University of Montreal. Dual tasks involving visual stimulus and a motor response (which he dubs 'in the eye and out the hand') and auditory stimulus with a verbal response (in the ear and out the mouth) can, with enough training, be performed as well together as apart. He speculates that the brain connections they use may be somehow special, because we learn to speak by hearing and learn to move by looking. But pair visual input with verbal response, or sound to motor, and there's no such dramatic improvement.”


解析：原文只提到Jolicoeur实验表明练习可以提高同时完成两个的任务的表现, 并未提及实验要在实验室内进行的相关信息, 因此答案为NOT GIVEN。

39

Research into phone use while driving has produced some surprising results.

正确答案： B. NO

Q39, NO

定位关键词：phone use


原文：F段, “Needless to say, there appear to be no researchers who believe that you can safely drive and have a phone conversation. And according to results or from a study at the University of Utah, even using a hands-free phone does not improve a driver's response time.”


解析：原文提到没有研究人员相信可以一边安全驾驶一边打电话, 并提到一项研究结果表明使用免提电话也无法增加驾驶员的反应时间。这说明实验结果证实了研究人员的观点，并没有令人感到惊讶, 所以答案为NO。


同义替换：phone use=phone conversation

40

Kramer's initial findings about cognitive abilities and aging confirm popular assumptions.

正确答案： A. YES

Q40, YES

定位关键词：Kramer's initial findings, cognitive abilities and aging


原文：F段, “And the general preconception that we tend to get worse at multitasking as we age is borne out. In one study conducted by Art Kramer at the University of Illinois, for example, both young and old participants were asked to do a simulated driving task while carrying on a conversation. He found that while younger drivers tended to miss background changes, older drivers failed to notice things that were highly relevant as well, and likewise had more trouble paying attention to the more important parts of the scene.”


解析：原文提到我们普遍认为随着年龄的增长, 我们同时处理多个任务时的能力越差。Kramer的研究证实这点。他发现相比年轻人, 老年人在一边聊天一边开车时无法注意到和驾驶高度相关的事物, 并且更难注意到更加重要的事物。因此答案为YES。


同义替换：cognitive abilities=notice things that were highly relevant /paying attention to the more important parts of the scene, confirm=is borne out, aging popular assumptions=general preconception
